items.q: Is the oxidation state of an atom the same as its actual charge?
items.correct: Not necessarily — oxidation state is an accounting tool assuming all bonds are fully ionic; real charge is usually smaller.
items.misconception: The oxidation state of an atom is exactly equal to its real ionic charge in every compound.
items.random_wrong: The oxidation state of an atom is always equal to the number of electrons it has.
items.why: Oxidation states are written with charge-like signs, so students treat them as actual charges, particularly in covalent compounds where this is misleading.

items.q: When an acid neutralises an alkali, what is the pH of the resulting solution?
items.correct: Approximately 7 if equivalent amounts of strong acid and strong alkali react — but it varies with strength.
items.misconception: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH exactly 7 regardless of the acid and base used.
items.random_wrong: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH 0.
items.why: Students learn the rule 'neutral = 7' so rigidly that they ignore weak-acid/weak-base cases where the salt is acidic or basic.

items.q: What colour is litmus paper in an alkali?
items.why: It's Blue. “Red” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Universal indicator turns purple at approximately pH:
items.why: The real figure is 13. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
